title: Field-driven phases in a three-dimensional twisted Kitaev model for CoNb$_2$O$_6$: Interplay of frustration and spin-orbit coupling
abstract: The Ising chain in a transverse field stands out as a paradigmatic example for a quantum phase transition. CoNb$_2$O$_6$ has been discussed as a material realization of this physics, but it was later realized that its magnetic exchange couplings are more complicated, taking the form of twisted Kitaev chains. Here we study a three-dimensional model for CoNb$_2$O$_6$, taking into account both Kitaev physics and frustrated inter-chain coupling, in applied magnetic field. Using semiclassical techniques at zero temperature, we map out the sequence of field-driven phases for arbitrary field direction; these include phases with commensurate and incommensurate inter-chain order. As a result of spin-orbit coupling, the phase diagram is extremely sensitive to small changes in the field angle. We compute static observables as well as magnetic excitation spectra in the various phases and connect our results to existing experimental data.
summary: This paper investigates the magnetic properties of the material CoNb2O6 using a 3D twisted Kitaev model. It explores how the competition between frustrated inter-chain interactions and spin-orbit coupling leads to a complex variety of magnetic phases. The study reveals that the phase sequence is highly sensitive to the angle of the applied magnetic field. The theoretical results, including magnetization and excitation spectra, are shown to be in good agreement with existing experimental observations.
main_problem: The study aims to understand the complex 3D magnetic phase diagram of CoNb2O6, specifically how the interplay of frustrated inter-chain coupling and spin-orbit coupling drives various field-induced phases.
main_result: The authors identify six distinct field-driven phases (including commensurate, incommensurate, and spin-flip phases) and demonstrate that the phase diagram is extremely sensitive to even minute changes in the applied magnetic field direction.
method: Semiclassical techniques and linear spin-wave theory are used at zero temperature to map the phase diagram and compute magnetic excitation spectra.
model_or_system: A 3D twisted Kitaev model for the magnetic insulator CoNb2O6, featuring Co2+ ions in zigzag chains with bond-dependent Ising interactions, frustrated inter-chain Heisenberg coupling, and an anisotropic g-tensor.
key_observables: Longitudinal and transverse magnetization, magnetic excitation spectra (magnon dispersion), dynamical structure factor, and magnetic Bragg peaks.
important_parameters: Inter-chain coupling strength (J_H,l), lattice distortion parameter (alpha), magnetic field magnitude and direction (theta, phi), and Kitaev coupling (K).
main_assumptions: The system is treated as an effective spin-1/2 model; dipole-dipole interactions are neglected; semiclassical spin-wave theory requires a manual rescaling of transverse field components to correct for the Ising limit.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the crystal structure; Figure 2 illustrates the twisted Kitaev chain; Figure 3 displays model ground states and spin structure factors; Figure 4 shows phase diagrams; Figures 8-10 present magnetization and dynamical structure factor results.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the 3D twisted Kitaev model and its Hamiltonian, proceeds to describe the semiclassical and spin-wave methods, presents the mapping of the field-driven phase diagram, compares results to experimental data, and concludes with discussions on phase sensitivity and fluctuations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Crystal structure of CoNb2O6: The Co2+ ions (blue) are surrounded by distorted edge-sharing octahedra of O2−
FIG. 2: Single twisted Kitaev chain with two local Ising axes ˆn(i) 1 , ˆn(i) 2 (magenta) alternating along the chain. The axis ˆn(i)

FIG. 3: Top: Model ground states for ⃗B applied in the ac plane. The crystallographic unit cell (light grey) contains two chains (circles) with two spins (arrows) each. Along each chain, the spins order ferromagnetically. Except for the paramagnetic high-field phase (not shown), all phases require an enlarged magnetic unit cell (dashed). The arrows represent the projection of the spins onto the ab plane; their c-axis component is color-coded. Bottom: Corresponding static spin structure factors P

FIG. 4: Model phase diagrams as function of field magnitude and direction, with phase labels and colors shown on the right. Columns (a-c) employ inter-chain Heisenberg couplings with JH,l = 0.05 meV and α = 0.6, whereas column (d) uses JH,l = 0.2 meV and α = 0.9, both with the intra-chain couplings by Konieczna et al. [28]. (a): ⃗B in the ab plane. (b): ⃗B in the bc plane. (c,d): ⃗B in the ac plane. The upper row shows the full angle and field range, while the lower row shows suitable zooms. The SF2 phase is seen to extend to rather large fields for Ω2 ≈60◦, 120◦; this is because Ω2 = 63.54◦, 116.46◦

FIG. 5: (a): Dispersion of the lowest magnon branch along Γ−Y in the high-field phase for different ⃗B ∥ˆb, computed with the intra-chain couplings by Konieczna et al. together with JH,l = 0.05 meV and α = 0.9. The dispersion minimum is at ⃗k = (0, k2,min, 0). (b): Magnon gap ωmin and corresponding wavevector k2,min as a function of B. The gap closes according to ωmin ∝(B −Bc)1/2, with Bc ≈5.84 T. At Bc we have k2,min ≈0.41, signalling the onset of the INC phase.

FIG. 6: Model phase diagrams as function of strength and direction of the applied field, focusing on nearly transverse fields in the bc plane near the transition into the high-field phase; Ω1 = 90◦corresponds to ⃗B ∥ˆb, i.e., transverse field. Results are shown for various choices of the inter-chain couplings α and JH,l; the intra-chain couplings are those of Ref. 28.

FIG. 7: (a,b,c): Transition fields into the high-field phase for different inter-chain coupling parameters JH,l as function of the isosceles distortion α; the intra-chain couplings are those of Ref. 28. (a) SF1↔P for ⃗B ∥ˆa, (b) SF2↔P for ⃗B ∥ˆc, (c) INC↔P for ⃗B ∥ˆb. Panel (d) shows the value of qinc at the INC↔P transition.

FIG. 8: Longitudinal (top) and transverse (bottom) components of the magnetization (color-coded) as a function of field magnitude and angle along two selected cuts, for model parameters JH,l = 0.05 meV and α = 0.6. (a,b): Ω2 = 0◦(bc plane). (c,d): Ω2 = 90◦(ab plane). Panels (a,c) show the full angle range, while panels (b,d) contain a zoom close to the transverse direction, Ω1 = 90◦. All phase transitions are of first order except for INC ↔P which is continuous for transverse field ⃗B ∥ˆb (white circle) but becomes weakly first order if ⃗B is tilted away from the b axis.

FIG. 9: Longitudinal magnetization (color-coded) as function of field direction as in Fig. 8, now covering a spherical segment near ⃗B ∥ˆb. (a): B = 2 T, for a range of 10◦tilt angle. (b): B = 5.57 T, for a range of 1◦tilt angle. Note that these images obey the point-group symmetries.

FIG. 10: Dynamical structure factor Sµν(⃗q, ω) for selected phases and fields, computed for the intra-chain couplings of [28] and JH,l = 0.05 meV and α = 0.6. The δ peaks were broadened into Gaussians with a width of η = 0.01 meV; the intensity of the diagonal components Sµµ is shown in a logarithmic color scale. (a): AF for B = 0. (b): SF1 for ⃗B ∥ˆa and B = 0.170 T. (c): SF1 for ⃗B ∥ˆc and B = 0.246 T. (d,e): INC for ⃗B ∥ˆb and B = 5.72 T, with (e) showing at low-energy zoom. (f): First Brillouin zone for the orthorhombic crystal system, together the path used to plot the excitation spectra [57]; note that this path consists of a connected piece (red) and disconnected ones (blue).
